channel.title: Insight on Early Reflections
channel.description: Recent content in Insight on Early Reflections

item.title: Insight as Skill
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: One of the elements I find most attractive in Rob Burbea’s approach to dharma practice is the way he defines insight in terms of effect rather than content. An insight, he says, is any way of looking at experience that releases clinging in the moment1. This carefully sidesteps a common but unhelpful tendency to view the meditative journey as a progression through a series of waystations defined as distinct experiential states, some of which promise to leave our perception of self and world altered forever. Instead the path is to be seen as something more like the acquisition of a bag of tricks, a gradual accumulation of heterogenous bits of know-how together yielding an ever greater capacity for skillful responsiveness.
item.content:encoded:
One of the elements I find most attractive in Rob Burbea’s approach to dharma practice is the way he defines insight in terms of effect rather than content. An insight, he says, is any way of looking at experience that releases clinging in the moment1. This carefully sidesteps a common but unhelpful tendency to view the meditative journey as a progression through a series of waystations defined as distinct experiential states, some of which promise to leave our perception of self and world altered forever. Instead the path is to be seen as something more like the acquisition of a bag of tricks, a gradual accumulation of heterogenous bits of know-how together yielding an ever greater capacity for skillful responsiveness.

All for the better insight, I’d say. Because one thing that is clear is that experiences are cheap these days, even (especially?) peak experiences. You can just fly to Peru and buy one. Or better, find some back-alley neurobiologist prepared to apply a carefully targeted electrical stimulus to your brain—contrary to expectation, ecstatic or mystical experiences have turned out to be some of the easiest to induce through direct electrical stimulation. And it is well-known that episodes of mania or psychosis are often accompanied by extremely strong experiential states of subjective revelation, even where the sense of revelation attaches to delusional beliefs. An even more dramatic example is found in Cotard’s delusion, a rare psychiatric condition in which the sufferer insists with absolute conviction that they are dead or do not exist (apparently with the same degree of self-certainty that led Descartes to insist that he did).

These examples are not intended to call into question the value of peak experiences, but they do cast doubt on their epistemic status. What they show is that there is something like a phenomenology of knowingness, a felt sense of knowing detachable from any particular known content, and which has no trouble at all attaching to false or even contradictory content. The implication of this detachability is that the mere fact of being in certain experiential state cannot in itself furnish the meditator with justification for any claims the experience might incline them to make about reality, the nature of consciousness, or anything else.

This is not to say that insight has no experiential component—certainly it does. But insight cannot simply be something one has. It must also be something one reflects upon, articulates, discusses with others, cross-references, practices, and ultimately consolidates as a kind of skill. What is required is something like a process of mutual calibration between first-person experience and third-person reflection, which could perhaps be likened to the training of a wine taster. No-one would claim it’s possible to become a proficient wine taster by merely learning about the chemistry of wine without ever actually tasting it. Yet the chemical knowledge does play a crucial role in the process, and cannot be dispensed with. By repeatedly and systematically matching chemical elements in the known composition of particular wines to particular flavours and aromas known experientially through tasting, the aspiring wine taster gradually learns to discern the presence of particular compounds and contrasts—they learn, for instance, to (quite literally) taste the phenolic content.

There is a sense in which the casual wine drinker tastes the phenolic content too, but only in the dim sense the phenolic content contributes to the overall flavour. They don’t taste the phenolic content as phenolic content; they are unable to pick it out as a distinct element in the mix. A point Paul Churchland once made about this is that it is not only that the experienced wine taster possesses a skill which most of us do not [1]. It seems also that through their acquisition of this skill the wine taster has in fact transformed the content of their experience. The experience of drinking wine is not to them what it is to the casual drinker, just as the astronomer’s experience of the night sky is different to the non-astronomer’s and the professional conductor hears something in the symphony most of us cannot. The conceptual discernment that comes through training makes possible a depth and richness of experience inaccessible without it.

Churchland then asks us to imagine a hypothetical future in which neurobiology has entered its ‘utopian’ era: our knowledge of the brain is complete and precise and technologies allowing us to look inside it to arbitrary levels of detail are cheap. He points out that in this situation we would in principle have the tools to each become the master tasters of our own inner lives, using those utopian brain sciences and technologies to train ourselves to reliably identify even the most subtle shadings of emotion in reference to the specific brain events they correlate with. Far from the devaluation of inner life many anticipate in the steady encroachment of neurobiology into the domain of first-person experience, according to Churchland the effect of this would be to open up vast new vistas of inner experience. Ultimately it would allow us to dispense with clumsy ‘folk psychological’ notions like boredom or grief, to be replaced by a neurobiological vocabulary enabling much finer discernments of conscious experience, and so, as with the astronomer looking up at the night sky, making possible new depths of appreciation, new modes of response.

Whatever your instincts may be with respect to this scenario, it makes vivid a certain interplay between theory and experience in the acquisition of a particular kind of skill, which necessarily brings with it a transformation of experience. This makes it helpful for thinking about the kind of transformation that insight is supposed to induce and how it might work. Insight practices are certainly not about mapping experiential qualities onto neurological correlates. They are rather about discerning the workings of a distinctive process, one which is always active in consciousness and in the construction of perceptual contents, yet which is at the same time hidden from consciousness on account of its reflexive identification with those very contents. And it is for this reason that insight is usually understood as beginning with the cultivation of skillful disidentification, in one form or another.

What the present line of thought helps us to see is that insight, which in the Burbea framing has a kind of counterfactual structure—if this way of looking is applied, it brings about that loosening—can be regarded as a skillset developed in the course meditative experience rather than as an experiential state in itself, and moreover how this illuminates rather than obscures the potential of insight to radically transform our experience of self and world.

  1. See e.g. Chapter 4 of Seeing that Frees. [2] ↩︎

item.title: The Weird Materialism of Early Buddhism
item.author: sam.earlyreflections@gmail.com (Samuel Ludford)
item.description: A recurring theme in the Pali discourses is the so-called ‘reflections on the repulsiveness of the body,’ a set of practices whose prominence in the scriptures is difficult to square with modern sensibilities. A hefty chunk of the Satipatthana Sutta is devoted to these reflections, including a section on ‘cemetery contemplations’ in which the meditator is instructed to visualize the decomposition of their own corpse in gruesome detail. Similar examples are found elsewhere, such as this representative passage from the Kayagata-sati Sutta:
item.content:encoded:
A recurring theme in the Pali discourses is the so-called ‘reflections on the repulsiveness of the body,’ a set of practices whose prominence in the scriptures is difficult to square with modern sensibilities. A hefty chunk of the Satipatthana Sutta is devoted to these reflections, including a section on ‘cemetery contemplations’ in which the meditator is instructed to visualize the decomposition of their own corpse in gruesome detail. Similar examples are found elsewhere, such as this representative passage from the Kayagata-sati Sutta:

Furthermore, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, tendons, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, pleura, spleen, lungs, large intestines, small intestines, gorge, feces, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, skin-oil, saliva, mucus, fluid in the joints, urine.’ Just as if a sack with openings at both ends were full of various kinds of grain — wheat, rice, mung beans, kidney beans, sesame seeds, husked rice — and a man with good eyesight, pouring it out, were to reflect, ‘This is wheat. This is rice. These are mung beans. These are kidney beans. These are sesame seeds. This is husked rice’; in the same way, the monk reflects on this very body from the soles of the feet on up, from the crown of the head on down, surrounded by skin and full of various kinds of unclean things.

Not your average body scan. Sometimes modern dharma teachers present these as marginal practices intended only to address hindrances involving lust, erotic fantasy, or vanity. But this doesn’t really account for the prevalence of passages like these, which seem to indicate something far more central to mindfulness of the body, appearing more or less anywhere it is under consideration. A better explanation is that they reflect a tendency in early Buddhism to view the sensual world as something to be transcended in its entirety, in contrast with modern framings which stress how mindfulness can both moderate and enhance engagement with sensual pleasures. Yet this is still rather general and nebulous, and one wonders whether the sheer oddness of these passages isn’t concealing some deeper and more specific lesson.

What I’d like to think about is how this preoccupation with gore points to the function of materiality—or rather a certain encounter with materiality—in the process of insight. I can begin to unpack what I mean by that by turning to the familiar example of pain. During a sit an aversive reaction to pain may be skillfully handled by bringing a calm attention to the moment-to-moment sensory content in the painful area. Doing so reveals the pain to be something much less substantial than it at first seems to be: a flux of physical micro-sensations (of heat, pressure, etc), not unpleasant in themselves but which seem to float in an unpleasant atmosphere which is distinct from them. Something peculiar happens with this shift in perspective, a ‘defabrication’ effect (to use Rob Burbea’s helpful term) which exposes the pain as a conceptual imputation, an empty phenomenon no longer solid enough for the aversion to get a grip on. In this way the defabrication of pain into its components—the (material) micro-sensations and (mental) feeling tone around them—brings about a loosening of dukkha in the associated sensory region.

What’s significant here is that the pain is first naively encountered as a simple bodily sensation, but defabrication shows that this “material” experience was always constructed through mental imputation at the same time as revealing its true material content, which is then encountered as something unsuspected and strange. This is why this kind of defabrication is sometimes likened to the experience of repeating a word until it loses all meaning, the sound of it suddenly seeming naked and strange in the absence of interpretation which is usually automatic and unconscious.

This strangeness may not be so apparent in the case of pain, but it becomes vivid in the defabrication of more pleasurable sensations. When first experimenting with mindful eating it is common to experience an increase in pleasure, perhaps even a dramatic increase. But turning up the granularity of attention and tuning into the moment-to-moment sensations lying beneath this pleasure can produce a similar effect to that described above in the case of pain—the aura of pleasantness separates out from the physical mechanics of chewing, the erratic movements of the tongue, the soggy matter being transferred from place to place in the mouth, all of which presents all of a sudden with a raw materiality that feels alien and kind of disgusting. The effect is not dissimilar to watching some creaky surrealist stop animation, like a film of Jan Svankmajer, a certain uncanny fascination. There’s a skeleton inside you and that creep is eating.

Insight experiences like these hint at what the reflections on the repulsiveness of the body may be alluding to. In naive or deluded consciousness what appears as a simple bodily sensation is in fact always a fabrication based on the intermingling of two distinct factors: materiality and mental imputation (rupa and nama). What the untangling of these two factors in insight experience provokes is an encounter with the material factor in its pure materiality, which is to say in the form of a radical otherness. The significance of repulsiveness, on this reading, is not so much to indicate a negative valuation of the body and its sensuality as that it encourages an encounter with the body in its fundamental otherness. This is not just otherness in the negative sense of being not-me, but a positive otherness which actively punctures and disrupts the field of conceptual imputation.

To look at it this way is to find in these early Buddhist practices a certain latent materialism. I use the word ‘materialism’ here because it is concerned with the inescapability of otherness in our contact with reality. Once we get past the substantialist tendency to categorise metaphysical positions on the basis of what kind of stuff they think reality is made of, the difference between materialism and idealism shows up ultimately as a disagreement about the status of otherness. The idealist idea that mind is fundamental to reality amounts to a promise that reality is something which is in an important sense already familiar to us; its task is then to account for the appearance of illusory otherness as an effect of dissociation, or the estrangement of consciousness from itself. But for the materialist otherness is irreducible and non-illusory, a basic facet of reality. Matter is just the name we give to this otherness, which manifests in the first place as the resistance reality puts up to our plans, to our theories, and to our unconscious mental imputations. Matter is the aspect of reality which pushes back.

Clearly, to read this kind of view back into early Buddhism is anachronistic at best, and I’m not claiming that early Buddhists were metaphysical materialists. The point of this is just to suggest that reading the tradition through the lens of materialism—something which in my experience many modern Buddhists in the West are extremely hostile to—can help to extract surprising lessons from parts of the Dharma that may otherwise remain puzzling.
